perturbation.original_text: If the Confidential Information is disclosed orally or visually, it shall be identified as such at the time of disclosure and confirmed in a writing to the recipient within thirty (30) days of such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Confidential Information is disclosed orally or visually, it shall be identified as such at the time of disclosure and confirmed in a writing to the recipient within sixty (60) days of such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confirmation period from 30 days to 60 days could be problematic if a state law mandates a shorter timeframe for confirming confidential information disclosures to maintain trade secret status or enforce non-disclosure obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) - varies by state adoption, example: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UTSA doesn't explicitly define a deadline for written confirmation of oral disclosures, extending the timeframe to 60 days increases the risk that a court might find the information wasn't adequately protected, potentially jeopardizing its status as a trade secret. Delaying confirmation can be seen as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy, undermining a claim for trade secret misappropriation if reasonable measures are not followed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.6 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.6. An action for misappropriation must be brought within three years after the misappropriation is discovered or by the exercise of reasonable diligence should have been discovered. For the purposes of this section, a continuing misappropriation constitutes a single claim. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 30 to 60 days for confirming confidential information disclosures introduces a potential legal contradiction. The Law Explanation argues that extending the timeframe increases the risk of jeopardizing the information's trade secret status due to a failure to take reasonable measures to protect its secrecy. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Code, CIV 3426.6, focuses on the statute of limitations for misappropriation actions (3 years), the Perturbation Explanation suggests that a state law might mandate a shorter timeframe for confirming confidential information disclosures. While this specific timeframe isn't explicitly stated in the provided snippets, the change in timeframe creates an inconsistency that could undermine trade secret protection, aligning with the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall govern all communications between the parties that are made during the period from the effective date of this Agreement to the date on which either party receives from the other written notice that subsequent communications shall not be so governed, provided, however, that each party's obligations under Sections 2 and 3 with respect to Confidential Information of the other party which it has previously received shall continue unless and until such Confidential Information falls within Section 4.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall govern all communications between the parties that are made during the period from the effective date of this Agreement to the date on which either party receives from the other written notice that subsequent communications shall not be so governed, provided, however, that each party's obligations under Sections 2 and 3 with respect to Confidential Information of the other party which it has previously received shall continue for a period of one (1) year unless and until such Confidential Information falls within Section 4.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality obligations to one year may conflict with the intended duration of trade secret protection, which can last indefinitely as long as the information remains a trade secret and is actively protected. Some confidential information may require more protection than a year, depending on the industry.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) - varies by state adoption, example: Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and the owner takes reasonable measures to maintain their secrecy. A one-year limitation on confidentiality obligations, regardless of whether the information remains a trade secret, directly contradicts the open-ended protection afforded by trade secret law. If the information still qualifies as a trade secret after one year, the contract's limitation could prevent the disclosing party from enforcing its trade secret rights.

Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) âImproper meansâ shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a one-year limitation on confidentiality obligations, which directly contradicts the principles of trade secret law as trade secrets are protected indefinitely as long as they remain secret and the owner takes reasonable measures to maintain their secrecy. The original text provides that 'each party's obligations under Sections 2 and 3 with respect to Confidential Information of the other party which it has previously received shall continue unless and until such Confidential Information falls within Section 4', which clearly stipulates an indefinite protection period. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' which involves 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By limiting the obligation to one year, the changed text allows for potential misappropriation after the one-year period even if the information still qualifies as a trade secret, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Except as set forth in this Section 2, all Confidential Information shall be in tangible form and shall be marked as Confidential or proprietary information of the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information must be in tangible form and may not be marked as Confidential or proprietary information of the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that information 'may not' be marked as confidential the agreement would cause the party to be noncompliant with laws protecting trade secrets or confidential information, which relies on businesses taking reasonable precautions to keep information confidential.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act addresses the theft of trade secrets related to products or services used in or placed in interstate commerce. While the act does not outright require marking, refusing to mark confidential information is failure to reasonably protect the information, which could potentially limit legal remedies under this act should the information be stolen or misappropriated.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that confidential information 'shall be marked,' imposing a requirement to mark it. The changed text states confidential information 'may not be marked,' which directly contradicts the original requirement and undermines reasonable precautions for trade secret protection. The Law Explanation states that refusing to mark confidential information could limit legal remedies under the Economic Espionage Act, because it would be a failure to reasonably protect the information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, reinforcing the relevance of this act to trade secrets protection. The change creates a contradiction because it actively prohibits a practice that helps establish and maintain confidentiality, potentially jeopardizing legal recourse for misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties agrees that it shall treat all Confidential Information of the other party with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information, and each of the parties represents that it exercises reasonable care to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties agrees that it shall treat all Confidential Information of the other party with minimal care, and each of the parties represents that it exercises minimal care to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'reasonable care' to 'minimal care' is non-compliant, as reasonable care is required to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: To qualify for trade secret protection under the UTSA, information must be subject to efforts that are 'reasonable under the circumstances' to maintain its secrecy. 'Minimal care' would almost certainly be deemed insufficient, negating trade secret status and preventing legal recourse for misappropriation. This contradiction undermines the very purpose of the NDA regarding confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'reasonable care' with 'minimal care' in protecting confidential information. The Law Explanation specifies that 'reasonable under the circumstances' is necessary for trade secret protection under the UTSA, and 'minimal care' would likely be insufficient. The second scraped law snippet describes the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) as a model law designed to resolve inconsistencies in trade secret protection across different states, indicating the importance of maintaining a standard of care. By reducing the standard of care to 'minimal,' the changed text contradicts the requirement of 'reasonable' efforts to maintain secrecy, potentially negating trade secret status and thus creating a legal contradiction.
